entry.title: Fiori Generator error: "Edmx metadata is required" using Reentrance Ticket
entry.content:
Hi SAP Experts,

I am encountering an issue while trying to generate a SAP Fiori application (List Report Page) using the @SAP/fiori:app generator in VS Code.

Environment Details:

  • Target System: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

  • System Type Selected: ABAP Environment on SAP Business Technology Platform

  • Authentication Method: Use Reentrance Ticket

Steps to Reproduce:

  1. Open SAP Fiori generator in VS Code.

  2. Select "List Report Page" template.

  3. In Data Source step, choose "Connect to a System" -> "New System".

  4. Set System Type to "ABAP Environment on SAP Business Technology Platform" and Definition Source to "Use Reentrance Ticket".

  5. Enter the System URL and click Next.

Observed Behavior: As soon as I click the Next button, the Application Wizard immediately crashes and throws the following error message (as shown in the attached screenshot):

@sap/fiori:app generator failed - env.on(error) An error has occurred, please restart the generator. : Error: An error has occurred, please restart the generator. : Edmx metadata is required to continue with generation. Application generation exiting.

What I have checked:

  1. The browser popup didn't seem to block any authentication process, but the tool didn't route me further to fetch the service list.

  2. It seems the generator failed to fetch the Edmx metadata from the backend system via the Reentrance Ticket flow.

Could you please guide me on how to resolve this? Is there a specific communication scenario, catalog service permission, or Business Role missing for my business user in the S/4HANA Cloud system to allow metadata fetching via Reentrance Ticket?

Thank you in advance for your help!

Only piece missing is for secured on-prem artifactory repositories need to supply the access token in .cicd_npmrc as below.

//<artifactory host>:<port>/path to the repo/:_authToken=${ACCESS_TOKEN}

create a secret text credential in CI/CD and add ACCESS_TOKEN credential to the Job configuration similar to CONN_PROXY.

entry.title: the SuccessFactors MDF object cust_ITA through SAP CPI using Upsert operation
entry.content:
When updating different fields of the same employee through multiple CPI upserts on different effective dates, the latest MDF record displays only the fields updated for that effective date. Previously populated fields are not carried forward to the latest record although the upserts are successful and historical records are available.

Business Requirement:
The latest ITA record should always display all available values for the employee, including:

  • Email
  • NTID
  • SIM
  • Laptop
  • Other Asset Information

Scenario:

Step 1:
A closed ticket is received from the source system (MYIB) for Email and NTID.

Upsert Payload:

<cust_ITA>
<cust_ITA>
2026-06-03
11000168
<cust_ITA_E_mail>Test@Test.com</cust_ITA_E_mail>
<cust_ITA_NTID>PRIYA.TEST</cust_ITA_NTID>
</cust_ITA>
</cust_ITA>

Result:
Record successfully upserted.

entry.title: Backdated inventory stock report - ALV report
entry.content:
Using ALV abap code I want to extract backadated inventory stock report 

SAP ABAP ALV report in SAP S/4HANA to display the material stock as on a selected backdated date using standard SAP tables.  
 
TablePurpose
MARDHHistorical storage location stock (Previous period stock, Monthly historical stock)
MBEWHHistorical valuation data(Historical Stock value, Std Price history)
MCHBHHistorical batch stock(Historical batch quantities)
MKPFMaterial document header
MSEGMaterial document items (ECC)
MATDOCMaterial movements (S/4HANA)
MARAGeneral Material Data
MAKTMaterial Description
 
  The report should calculate and display the historical stock position of materials based on the user-selected date range.  
 
Selection Screen Input: 
1. Material
2. Plant
3. Storage Location
4. Batch 
5. Valuation Type.
6. Special stock Indicator
7. Selection date range (From and To Date)
 
Output ALV Field:
1. Material
2. Material Description,
3. Plant
4. Storage Location
5. Batch
6. Valuation Type.
7. Unit of Measure
8. Opening Stock 
9. Closing Stock

entry.title: Re: Cannot migrate AuC during fiscal year
entry.content: Dear @MichalPalecko1 I followed your steps and error still occurred. Here is more details about AUC, Cost in AUC is on January 6 2026. So, asset capitalization date in asset master file = January 6 2026, Posting Date in the Document (asset posting transaction header tab) = April 30, 2026, Reference Date (asset posting transaction item tab) = April 30, 2026 with asset transaction type = 900

entry.title: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 09 - Injecting conditions via filters
entry.content:
When modelling with CDS and its family of languages we have access to a wide selection of clean and concise declarative syntax. In the expressions area there are expressions (and in particular path expressions) and also a certain style of filter.

These filters can be used in various places and are a great example of how CDS models elevate us from underlying SQL, up to a domain model layer, where intent can be expressed more simply (and less technically).

Here's how this special filter is defined:

And here are some examples of how it's used (and thus also what it "looks like").

In an exists predicate:

SELECT from Authors { name }
  where exists books[stock > 100]

In a from clause:

SELECT from Books[price > 19.99]
  { author.name as name }

There's a path expression in that example too, and talking of path expressions, it can be used between segments of a path in such path expressions:

SELECT from Books
  { title, author[age < 40].name as author }

This type of filter has a name denoting where it appears. What is that name (the name is a single word, and let's agree to write it all in lower case)?

This is a question from the June Developer Challenge on CAP Knowledge. And don't forget: always submit your answer as a hash, on its own - read the Taking part section of the intro post for more info. At the end of today, this question will be updated with links to further reading on this topic.

entry.title: Clarification on data monitor tasks when running consolidation on a yearly basis
entry.content:
As part of an SAP S/4 HANA Cloud, Public Edition greenfield implementation, we have a requirement where the financial consolidation using Group Reporting is expected to be executed only once after the year end closure. We have defined and assigned a new process period cycle M_YER (Fiscal Year Independent) since there's no SAP pre-delivered consolidation process period cycle for yearly consolidation. We have integrated companies as well as spreadsheet companies in our consolidation group but no currency conversion or consolidation of investments is involved. Looking for clarity on the below since there's nothing in the SAP Help documentation.

 

1. Since the Data Monitor inherits values from the Set Global Parameters app, it only has single Fiscal Year and Period selection and does not allow Process Year Period selection. In our scenario, where we want to execute consolidation for the entire year and not every month, would we still need to run the tasks in the Data Monitor for each of the Fiscal Year Periods individually?

E.g. Execute the tasks in the data monitor for Fiscal Year 2026 Period 1, then period 2 and so on till period 12?

entry.title: Re: NEW COMPANY CODE CREATION WITH DIFFERANT CURRENCY IN...
entry.content:
HI Sandeep,

you can create new company code for UAE in CBC client. If the scope items are same as Indian company code , you need to goto CBC, click on Organization structure give you country and company code similar to how you performed for Indian Company code.

If you have to add any additional scope item to be activated for UAE business, then you need to first add the Scope item. In the above screen there is Scope under Activities on left hand side. Activate your scope. then move to Primary finance settings. No change required in primary finance settings because the currency for 0L and FYV will remain same for UAE company. Then go to Organization Structure create UAE company code and proceed.

In case you need to perform separate testing and test client is required for UAE, the you have to use Parallel Line approach.

Reports without subreports run successfully. We have upgraded from SP35 to SP40. The login dialog consistently shows: Server Name = DESKTOP-94TBRUD\MSSQLSERVER01 Database = blank We are using: * ReportClientDocument * DatabaseController.LogonEx * DatabaseController.ReplaceConnection * SetTableLocation * ApplyLogOnInfo Logging confirms that all main report and subreport connections are changed to BRPSQL1 / Meat_Plant. Despite this, Crystal still prompts for DESKTOP-94TBRUD\MSSQLSERVER01 whenever a report contains a subreport. Is there a subreport connection object or metadata source that is not exposed through Database.Tables, DataSourceConnections, or GetConnectionInfos() that could still be triggering the login dialog?

entry.title: Automate Recurring Data Updates in SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

In a recent demo scenario, there was a need to automatically update product inventory records in SAP HANA Cloud when they had not been refreshed for more than 30 days. This is a common pattern for housekeeping and status management, and it can be handled neatly using the built-in scheduler in SAP HANA Cloud through SAP HANA Database Explorer.

For the scheduler syntax and options, refer to SAP Help: Create Scheduler Job Statement.

Assumed PRODUCT_INVENTORY Table Schema

For this example, assume there is a PRODUCT_INVENTORY table with the following structure:

CREATE COLUMN TABLE PRODUCT_INVENTORY (
    PRODUCT_ID NVARCHAR(20) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    PRODUCT_NAME NVARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    QUANTITY INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    INVENTORY_STATUS NVARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'ACTIVE', -- 'ACTIVE', 'OUTDATED'
    UPDATED_AT TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

The requirement is to mark records as OUTDATED when their UPDATED_AT value is older than 30 days and the current status is ACTIVE.

Use-case

Assume there is a PRODUCT_INVENTORY table and the requirement is to mark records as OUTDATED when their UPDATED_AT value is older than 30 days. This approach keeps the logic inside the database and works well for simple recurring operational tasks.

Step 1: Create the procedure

CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PR_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY_STATUS ()
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY INVOKER
AS
BEGIN
    UPDATE <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PRODUCT_INVENTORY
       SET INVENTORY_STATUS = 'OUTDATED',
           UPDATED_AT = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
     WHERE INVENTORY_STATUS = 'ACTIVE'
       AND UPDATED_AT < ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, -30);
END;

Step 2: Schedule the procedure

In SAP HANA Cloud, job scheduling is done with CREATE SCHEDULER JOB. The HANA Cloud cron format uses a 7-field pattern:

Year, Month, Day, DayOfWeek, Hour, Minute, Second

To run the procedure every day at midnight, use:

CREATE SCHEDULER JOB 
<YOUR_SCHEMA>.JOB_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY
CRON '* * * * 0 0 0'
ENABLE
PROCEDURE <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PR_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY_STATUS;

Run the procedure on demand

If you want to execute the procedure manually instead of waiting for the scheduled job, you can run it directly in SAP HANA Database Explorer using the CALL statement. This is useful for testing the procedure logic before enabling the scheduler or for triggering an immediate update when needed.

CALL <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PR_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY_STATUS();

Monitor the job

After creating the job, you can verify it using the scheduler monitoring views in HANA Cloud. The scheduler metadata is available in M_SCHEDULER_JOBS, and the correct job-name column is SCHEDULER_JOB_NAME.

SELECT *
FROM M_SCHEDULER_JOBS
WHERE SCHEDULER_JOB_NAME = 'JOB_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY';

Clean-up

If the procedure and scheduler job are no longer needed, they can be removed with standard SQL statements in SAP HANA Cloud. Use DROP SCHEDULER JOB to delete the scheduled job and DROP PROCEDURE to remove the stored procedure.

DROP SCHEDULER JOB <YOUR_SCHEMA>.JOB_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY;

DROP PROCEDURE <YOUR_SCHEMA>.PR_UPDATE_PRODUCT_INVENTORY_STATUS;

If needed, you can verify the objects are gone by checking M_SCHEDULER_JOBS and the procedure catalog views afterward.

Alternative scheduling options

For scenarios where scheduling should be managed outside the database, these are common alternatives on SAP BTP:

  • SAP Integration Suite - Timer Event
    Useful when the database update is one step in a broader integration or orchestration flow.
  • SAP Job Scheduling Service
    Suitable for centralized background job scheduling across BTP applications and services.
  • SAP Automation Pilot Service
    Commands for infrastructure operations can be scheduled within the SAP Automation Pilot, making it a good fit for maintenance and capacity management scenarios, for example: starting and stopping an SAP HANA Cloud instance at scheduled times.

Key Takeaways

For lightweight recurring database updates, the built-in SAP HANA Cloud scheduler is the simplest option because the logic stays inside HANA Cloud and is easy to maintain and monitor using SAP HANA Database Explorer. If the process spans multiple systems or requires platform-level orchestration, consider using SAP Integration Suite Timer Event, SAP Job Scheduling Service, or a custom CAP-based approach. For enterprise applications, CAP is often the recommended approach because it allows you to trigger CAP REST APIs on specific schedules or cron expressions without tying up your app’s core resources.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 09 - Injecting conditions via filters
Blog#4: How to change fields to "Display-Only" in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
In my last post, I shared how mandatory fields in SAP Cloud ALM can be enforced. The next logical step is to talk about how to lock down fields once they should no longer be changed.

It is a risk that almost every team will face. A Requirement is refined, scope and effort are agreed, the approval is signed off. And then, days later, somebody quietly changes the Scope or the Effort. The plan that was approved no longer matches the data in the system.


Let's be specific: Scope, Effort and Company Code locked after approval

Here is a concrete example from one of my projects. We open a Requirement in status "In Refinement" and scroll down to the "Additional Information" area. All the fields are editable, the Scope, the Company Code, the Priority, the Effort. So far, that is exactly how it should be. The Requirement is still being shaped.

Status is "In Refinement" - Fields are still editable


Then we set the approval status to "Ready for Approval" and save. The Requirement enters the approval process. And as soon as we save, the Scope, the Effort and the Company Code are no longer editable. The fields are still visible and the data is still there, but nobody can change the values anymore.

Status is "In Approval" - Fields are 'Display-Only'

 

This stays in place across all following statuses. When the Requirement is approved, the same fields remain read-only. What was agreed at approval, stays as approved.

More than approval status: lock fields for specific user groups

The example above locks fields based on the approval status. The same approach also works to restrict editing by user groups. Through SAP BTP role collections, we can decide who is actually allowed to edit a specific field, and make it display-only for everyone else.

A typical case: the Effort on a Requirement should only be editable by Project Managers, not by everyone in the team. A rule can be defined that makes the Effort read-only for all users who are not assigned to the "Project Manager" role collection (could also be a custom BTP role collection). The field is still visible for the whole team, the planning information stays transparent, but only the right people can change the actual value.

This also works in combination with the status-based rule from above. Effort can be editable only by Project Managers while a Requirement is in refinement, and as soon as the Requirement enters the approval process, the field becomes read-only for everyone.

How it is configured, without code

The "Display-Only" rules are configured with a graphical editor. No developer required.

In the pony administration under the "Rules" tile, we see different rule types. The one we just looked at is "Make Field Read Only". We click Edit on the rule and find the same three-step structure as for the mandatory field check:

  • Step 1 - Which fields should become read-only: in our case the Scope, the Effort and the Company Code
  • Step 2 - Under which conditions the rule applies: in our case when the Requirement status is "In Approval" or any status after the approval
  • Step 3 - Who is affected: the rule can apply to all users, or be restricted to a specific user group through the SAP BTP role collection assigned to them (for example, only Project Managers can edit the field)

Details of a rule

 

On the right side of the editor, a summary of the rule explains the structure once more. A click on Apply and the rule is active. From this moment on, the listed fields can no longer be changed in a Requirement that has reached the approval process.

 

In summary: you can decide exactly when and for whom a field in SAP Cloud ALM should be locked down. The data stays visible and the history stays intact, but the values can only be changed by the right people, at the right moment in the process. Read-only is no longer a hope. It is a rule.

Thanks for reading. If you have your own "the field was changed after approval and nobody noticed" story, drop it in the comments.

In my next post, I will share how pony hides unused functionalities in SAP Cloud ALM, so the UI shows only what your team actually needs.

entry.title: SAP's AI-Native North Star Architecture: Building the Autonomous Enterprise
entry.content:
SAP recently published its AI-Native North Star Architecture paper — a 36-page strategic document that outlines how SAP envisions the future of enterprise software. This is not a product announcement or a release roadmap. It is a reference architecture describing the direction SAP is taking across all its products and platforms as AI becomes central to how businesses operate.

This blog post summarizes the key ideas, architecture layers, and concepts from the paper so SAP community members can understand what this means for their organizations and how to start thinking about it.

 

What is the North Star Architecture?

A strategic vision document from SAP's Office of the CTO (OCTO)

Outlines how AI, agents, data, and platform capabilities will converge in SAP systems

Not a product commitment or delivery roadmap — it sets strategic direction

Published on the SAP Architecture Center and updated quarterly

Document owners: PVN PavanKumar and Anirban Majumdar (Head of OCTO, SAP SE)

 

 

The Big Picture: Why This Architecture Matters Now

For decades, enterprise software has been a system of record. It stores transactions, enforces rules, and tells you what happened. But it cannot tell you why decisions were made, what alternatives were considered, or what to do next. That reasoning has always lived in people — scattered across emails, meetings, and tribal knowledge.

SAP believes AI makes it possible to build a system of context on top of the system of record. A connected layer where enterprise data, process knowledge, and decision history are linked together so that AI agents can reason across them — not just retrieve from them.

The scale of the opportunity is significant. AI is expected to add trillions of dollars to global GDP over the coming decade. Enterprise AI software is projected to grow into hundreds of billions in annual value. More importantly, the nature of AI-driven work is changing: tasks that take minutes today are on a path toward running for a full human workday autonomously within a few years.

 

Key Insight from the Paper

"In enterprise AI, context is the decisive advantage."

Frontier models are becoming commoditized — every vendor can access the same leading models.

What varies is the context those models reason over.

SAP has five decades of business processes, master data, and domain knowledge.

That context is the strategic moat — and no model can replicate what it has never seen.

 

 

From AI-First to AI-Native: What Is the Difference?

This distinction is at the heart of the paper and worth understanding clearly because it changes how you think about what SAP products will look like in the coming years.

 

 

AI-First

In an AI-first world, AI is a feature added to an existing application. An AI assistant in your ERP can summarize an invoice or suggest a journal entry. But it is bounded by that application. It cannot see that the same supplier has open disputes in service management, delayed shipments in logistics, and a renegotiated contract in procurement — because those facts live in separate systems with no shared understanding.

SAP describes three barriers that keep AI-first limited: AI systems lack business and process context (leading to unreliable answers), they sit on disconnected systems without a shared data model, and they lack the governance needed to be accountable at scale.

AI-Native

AI-native solves all three. Software operates across landscape boundaries as a system of context. Every interaction feeds intelligence. Every correction becomes a learning signal. The system continuously improves. The delivery model shifts from software as a service to outcome as a service.

 

 

The Architecture: Four Layers Working Together

The AI-native North Star architecture is built on four reimagined layers. Together, they form what SAP calls the Cognitive Core — an enterprise intelligence system that integrates SAP's business data, process knowledge, and reasoning models to enable systems that learn and adapt.

 

 

  1. User Experience Layer

Today, users navigate between applications, follow predefined workflows, and manually piece together information across systems. In the AI-native model, users simply state their intent and the system assembles the experience around them.

The central component here is Joule Work — SAP's unified entry point that interprets intent across natural language, voice, and multimodal inputs. SAP describes five experience modes that Joule will support:

  • Discover — a personalized overview that learns over time
  • Conversations — ask, search, act, and get insights
  • Spaces — dynamic work environments assembled on the fly
  • Jobs — making agentic work traceable for business users
  • Develop — turning business intents into agentic solutions

 

Looking ahead, SAP also mentions Physical AI — Joule moving beyond the screen into robotics and smart glasses, bringing SAP business logic into the physical world.

  1. Process Layer

The process layer is where business logic is defined. In the AI-native model, applications become capability providers for agents rather than the primary executors of work. A procurement agent, for example, can orchestrate across ERP, sourcing, supplier management, and third-party systems as a single domain-aware participant.

Agents follow a reason-act-observe loop: they decompose goals, execute actions using tools, and refine based on results. SAP supports building solutions across a spectrum from fully deterministic apps to open-ended agents with broader autonomy.

All agent development comes together in Joule Studio — the unified design-time environment. Developers can go from business intent to code, following a flow from requirements document through specification to deployment.

  1. Foundation Layer (AI and Data)

This is where data and AI come together as the intelligent core. SAP describes this layer as evolving from a system of record to a system of context — where data, process knowledge, and decision history are connected and compounding.

Three components define this layer:

  • SAP Business Data Cloud — connects data from across the enterprise into a single governed landscape, with data products as the primary interface between SAP data and the agents, dashboards, and AI systems that consume it
  • SAP Knowledge Graph — the semantic backbone of the architecture. It links natural language inputs to SAP's structured metadata, opening access to tens of thousands of APIs and data models. When a user asks "show me overdue orders," the Knowledge Graph finds the right API, identifies correct parameters, and constructs an accurate query
  • Model Services — SAP-trained domain models (including RPT-1 for structured business data), plus access to third-party frontier LLMs through a generative AI hub

 

One standout insight from the paper: "Data was the moat of the last decade. Context is the moat of the next."

  1. Platform Layer

The platform layer is where applications, agents, and workflows run. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) provides composable services across data management, application development, integration, and security.

In the AI-native model, BTP evolves from hosting applications to running SAP-managed enterprise agents across the customer landscape. The platform provides:

  • Managed agent runtime with sandboxing, identity, memory, and observability built in
  • Agent harness — the governance layer that wraps models with enterprise controls
  • Agentic SDKs for building agents in any framework
  • Observability built on Open Telemetry covering agent execution end to end

 

A key insight from the paper: "The model reasons. The harness governs. The harness, not the model, determines the ceiling."

 

The Autonomous Enterprise: SAP's Vision for the Future

The Autonomous Enterprise is SAP's term for the destination this architecture is building toward. It describes enterprises that respond more quickly, operate more intelligently, and perform better by design — using AI assistants and agents to manage and execute processes across domains in one connected, adaptive system.

 

 

 

Element

What It Does

Joule

Central engagement layer bringing together data, workflows, and agents across all SAP systems and beyond

SAP Autonomous Suite

AI agents executing work across Finance, Spend, SCM, HCM, and Customer Experience

Industry AI Portfolio

Built-in vertical process knowledge, domain-specific data models, and regulatory logic

SAP Business AI Platform

Unifies SAP BDC, BTP, Business Transformation Management, and AI Foundation for enterprise-grade governance

RISE/GROW with SAP

Accelerated adoption path for the Autonomous Enterprise through agent-led toolchains

 

The Data Flywheel: Why Context Compounds

One of the most important conceptual contributions of the paper is the idea of the data flywheel. Within the four-layer architecture, data informs decisions, decisions improve processes, and better processes generate richer data. This is a self-reinforcing loop that creates a compounding advantage over time.

 

 

This is why SAP argues that the Autonomous Enterprise is not just a faster version of today's ERP. It is a qualitatively different kind of system — one that gets better with every interaction, every correction, and every resolved exception. The value compounds in a way that static software cannot.

 

 

Integration, Security, Ethics and Governance

Integration: From Connector-Driven to Intelligence-Driven

SAP describes integration itself as shifting from connector-driven to intelligence-driven. In the AI-native model, agents dynamically determine intent, select the right APIs, and orchestrate calls across systems — rather than relying on pre-built point-to-point connectors.

Two open protocols underpin this:

  • Model Context Protocol (MCP) — provides tool integration, enabling agents to discover and invoke capabilities across systems
  • Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol — supports multi-agent coordination, enabling agents built by different teams, partners, and customers to collaborate within governed boundaries

Security and Agent Identity

Agentic AI introduces new security risks that traditional frameworks were not designed to handle. Agents make autonomous decisions, access external tools, and operate across trust boundaries. SAP's response is to treat agents as first-class principals — they authenticate, receive authorizations, and are governed like any other enterprise actor.

SAP describes a three-tier AI defense architecture: a Foundation tier (zero-trust authentication, prompt-injection detection), an Automation tier (AI agents that scale security operations), and a Supervision tier (deterministic control agents that monitor and authorize other agents).

AI Ethics and Governance

The paper gives significant attention to AI ethics, treating it as a foundational cross-cutting concern rather than a compliance checkbox. SAP applies a structured AI ethics impact assessment to all use cases, classifying them by risk. High-risk use cases escalate to an AI ethics steering committee and, when necessary, to the SAP Executive Board.

SAP's ethics approach is aligned with UNESCO's Recommendation on the Ethics of AI and the OECD AI Principles. The paper is clear: when no ethically acceptable trade-off can be found, the AI system cannot proceed in that form.

 

 

The Ecosystem: Marketplace for Agents, Tools and Extensions

The AI-native architecture is designed to extend beyond SAP. The SAP AI Agent Hub serves as the central marketplace where agents, data products, and tools are published, evaluated, and composed into solutions. The full agent lifecycle is managed here — from planning and development through discovery, provisioning, observability, governance, and optimization.

Partners can build and publish domain-specific agents — logistics optimization, regulatory compliance, demand forecasting — with standardized metadata, quality metrics, and trust ratings. Customers can extend SAP-built agents with their own business rules, custom data sources, and industry-specific knowledge.

The result is a network effect: more agents → more capabilities → more builders → more value. The same platform dynamics that defined consumer technology are now being applied to enterprise intelligence.

 

 

Architecting the Quantum Era

The paper closes with a section on quantum computing — not as a near-term reality but as an architectural consideration SAP is already designing for. Quantum processing units (QPUs) will operate as mathematical coprocessors for complex optimization and simulation tasks, with outputs flowing back into deterministic workflows.

SAP describes a hybrid model where classical determinism, AI-enhanced cognition, and quantum probability work together within a trusted enterprise foundation — with post-quantum cryptography built in from the start.

 

 

Quick Reference: Key Terms from the Paper

If you are reading the full paper, these terms come up frequently. Here is a plain-language reference:

 

 

 

What This Means for SAP Customers and Professionals

The North Star Architecture is a vision document, not a product roadmap. But it signals clearly where SAP is heading and has several practical implications for organizations using SAP today.

 

Area

Implication for SAP Organizations

User Experience

Joule becomes the primary interface. Traditional Fiori apps remain but natural language and intent-driven UX will grow in importance

Process Design

Business processes will increasingly be described as agent capabilities rather than rigid workflow steps

Data Strategy

Data products and semantic data management become more important — garbage-in is even more costly when agents are reasoning over data

IT Architecture

Agent identity, governance, and observability need to be planned for early — not retrofitted

Skills

Understanding MCP, agent orchestration, context engineering, and semantic data modeling will be valuable

Governance

AI ethics and risk classification processes need to be established before agents are deployed in high-stakes workflows

entry.title: Re: Cost element in Standard Cost estimate not taking GBB...
entry.content:
It is not random. The movement type + value string configuration that decides whether ZBR or VBR is hit. The costing relevancy flag then determines whether the system tries to pull the account into the cost estimate. Since VBR isn’t mapped in your cost component structure, you get the error.

My recommendation:

  • First, check OMJJ for the movement types involved and see which value string they’re tied to.
  • If VBR is unavoidable for certain relevancy types, you’ll need to add the VBR cost element into your cost component structure.
  • If the business wants all such cases to hit ZBR, then the value string config (or a user exit) needs to be adjusted.

SAP HANA Cloud is a modern cloud database that helps organizations store, manage, and analyze data in real time. It provides powerful in-memory processing capabilities that enable faster application performance and better business decision-making.

In this blog, we will walk through the basics of working with SAP HANA Cloud, including database connectivity, table creation, data management, and SQL operations with practical examples.

Go to BTP Cockpit

 

 
Create HANA cloud instance
 

 

 
Add password and Whitelist IP so that the DB can be accessed from any IP address

Look into HANA tool application and wait for Hana DB instance to Runn

Create Table 
 

 

 
 
Insert data

entry.title: Freight Issue in GRN posting
entry.content:
While creating the PO in the PRD server, the user selected the freight condition in the second line item of the PO only. After posting the GRN for the first line item, no delivery cost was calculated, which is correct as per the pricing condition maintained in the PO.

However, while posting the GRN for the second line item with a quantity of 1,455, the GRN value is 181,875.00. Based on the freight condition maintained in the PO, the freight amount should be 3,637.50. However, the system is calculating a freight value of 6,453.00 instead of the expected 3,637.50.

The user requires clarification on how this discrepancy is occurring.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 09 - Injecting conditions via filters
Re: Backdated inventory stock report - ALV report
entry.content:
Hello @vanajakshi_raghavendra 

As you have mentioned MATDOC in your question, let me assume you are on S/4HANA already.

MARDH, MBEWH, MCHBH, MKPF, MSEG are not really DB tables anymore. They are simulated by replacement objects with MATDOC data. See the note 2206980 - Material Inventory Management: change of data model in S/4HANA So at the end of the day you get your data from MATDOC, even if you select from MKPF, MSEG, etc.

MARDH, MBEWH, MCHBH provide historical stock on monthly basis. You could get stock level at the end of a month from there and then calculate back to the exact date with MATDOC movement types.

You could also use Material Stock at Posting Date C_MaterialStockByKeyDate CDS view to get the data for your report.

Also consider SAP standard Stock for Posting Date report, transaction MB5B.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Building an AI-Powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator
entry.content:
Building an AI-Powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator

Introduction

In SAP integration projects, technical design documents are critical for capturing interface details, design decisions, mapping rules, adapter configurations, error handling, and operational monitoring. However, creating these documents manually for every SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CPI, interface can be time-consuming and repetitive.

This blog explains a practical use case for building an AI-powered application that can generate SAP CPI technical design documents automatically using inputs such as manual interface details, iFlow packages, and mapping sheets.

The goal is not to replace solution architects or developers, but to accelerate documentation creation and provide a consistent first draft that can be reviewed, edited, and approved by the project team.

Business Problem

SAP CPI developers often spend significant effort documenting integration flows after development is complete. A typical technical design document includes:

  • Interface overview
  • Source and target system details
  • Adapter configuration
  • Mapping and transformation logic
  • Error handling
  • Retry and reprocessing strategy
  • Monitoring and logging
  • Security and authentication
  • Testing scope
  • Deployment checklist

In large integration programs, the same documentation structure is repeated across many interfaces. This creates an opportunity to use AI to generate a structured draft based on available technical inputs.

Proposed Solution

The proposed solution is an AI-assisted SAP CPI TDD Generator.

The application allows users to enter basic interface details, upload an SAP CPI iFlow package, optionally attach a mapping sheet, and generate a technical design document in Word format.

The generated document can include:

  1. Technical design document content
  2. Architecture diagram
  3. Mapping sheet appendix
  4. Test scenarios
  5. Deployment checklist
  6. Assumptions and dependencies

The user can review and edit the generated output before downloading the final document.

High-Level Architecture

The application consists of the following components:

 

Key Features

  1. Manual TDD Generation

The user can provide basic information such as:

  • Interface name
  • Source system
  • Target system
  • Sender adapter
  • Receiver adapter
  • Mapping logic
  • Error handling
  • Integration flow description

The AI model uses this information to generate a structured technical design document.

  1. TDD Generation Using iFlow Package

The application can accept an exported SAP CPI iFlow package in ZIP format. The backend extracts relevant files such as:

  • .iflw
  • XML files
  • Groovy scripts
  • XSLT mappings
  • WSDL files
  • XSD files
  • Properties files

This extracted information is passed to the AI model to generate a more accurate document.

  1. Mapping Sheet Attachment

The user can upload a mapping sheet in Excel or CSV format. The application reads the mapping data and includes it in the generated Word document as an appendix.

This is useful for documenting source-to-target field-level transformations.

  1. Architecture Diagram Generation

The application generates a basic architecture diagram showing the integration flow:

Source System → Sender Adapter → SAP CPI → Mapping/Transformation → Receiver Adapter → Target System

It also includes monitoring, logging, and error handling as part of the diagram.

  1. Word Document Export

The final output is generated as a Word document. The document includes:

  • Interface details
  • Architecture diagram
  • AI-generated TDD content
  • Mapping sheet appendix

This makes the document easy to review, modify, and share with project stakeholders.

 

Technology Stack

A simple prototype can be built using the following technologies:

Component

Technology

Frontend

Streamlit

Backend

Python

AI Model Access

OpenRouter / OpenAI-compatible API

Document Generation

python-docx

Mapping Sheet Processing

pandas, openpyxl

Diagram Generation

Pillow

File Processing

zipfile

This stack is lightweight and suitable for a quick proof of concept.

Sample User Flow

  1. User opens the Streamlit application.
  2. User selects one of the generation options:
    • Manual Entry
    • Generate using iFlow Package
    • Generate using iFlow Package + Mapping Sheet
  3. User provides interface details.
  4. User uploads the iFlow ZIP file.
  5. User uploads the mapping sheet, if available.
  6. User clicks Generate TDD.
  7. The application extracts technical details.
  8. The AI model generates the TDD content.
  9. The user reviews or edits the generated document.
  10. The user downloads the final Word document.

Prompt Engineering Approach

The application uses a controlled prompt to reduce hallucination. The prompt instructs the AI model to behave as an SAP CPI Integration Architect and generate a technical design document using only the provided information.

Example instruction:

Use the iFlow package details where available.

Use the mapping sheet details where available.

Only use information provided by the user, iFlow package, or mapping sheet.

Do not invent missing information.

If any information is missing, mention "To be confirmed".

Do not include passwords, secrets, API keys, or credentials.

This approach helps ensure that the generated document remains reliable and does not include unsupported assumptions.

Generated TDD Sections

The generated technical design document can include the following sections:

  1. Document Purpose
  2. Interface Overview
  3. Business Requirement Summary
  4. Architecture Diagram Description
  5. Source System Details
  6. Target System Details
  7. SAP CPI iFlow Design
  8. Sender Adapter Configuration
  9. Receiver Adapter Configuration
  10. Message Mapping and Transformation Logic
  11. Routing Logic
  12. Exception Handling
  13. Retry and Reprocessing Strategy
  14. Security and Authentication
  15. Monitoring and Logging
  16. Externalized Parameters
  17. Mapping Sheet Summary
  18. Assumptions
  19. Dependencies
  20. Testing Scope
  21. Test Scenarios
  22. Deployment Checklist
  23. Appendix Reference

Benefits

This solution can help integration teams by:

  • Reducing manual documentation effort
  • Improving consistency across TDD documents
  • Accelerating handover from development to support
  • Creating a reusable documentation framework
  • Helping junior developers understand CPI interface design
  • Reducing missing sections in technical documentation
  • Enabling faster review cycles

Important Considerations

While AI can accelerate documentation, generated content should always be reviewed by a developer, solution architect, or technical lead.

The application should not expose or store sensitive information such as:

  • Passwords
  • API keys
  • Client secrets
  • Private certificates
  • Production credentials

For enterprise usage, additional controls should be added, such as:

  • Authentication and authorization
  • Audit logging
  • Secure file handling
  • Data masking
  • Approved AI model usage
  • Document versioning
  • Human approval workflow

Possible Enhancements

Future enhancements can include:

  • Direct connection to SAP Integration Suite APIs
  • Automatic extraction of adapter properties
  • Advanced iFlow XML parsing
  • Interface version comparison
  • Confluence integration
  • Jira integration
  • SAP BTP deployment
  • Approval workflow
  • Reusable client-specific TDD templates
  • Automated test case generation
  • Compliance checks against integration design standards

Conclusion

An AI-powered SAP CPI Technical Design Document Generator can significantly improve productivity in SAP integration projects. By combining SAP CPI artifact extraction, mapping sheet analysis, AI-generated documentation, architecture diagram generation, and Word export, teams can create high-quality first drafts of technical design documents in minutes.

The key principle is to use AI as an accelerator, not as the final authority. The best approach is:

Extract facts → Generate draft → Review and edit → Approve and publish

This use case demonstrates how AI can be practically applied in SAP Integration Suite projects to reduce repetitive effort and improve documentation quality.

Source Code

GIT Repository : https://github.com/jacksnh/sap-cpi-ai-tdd-generator

entry.title: Modern ABAP Control Break Processing Using LOOP AT GROUP BY and REDUCE Operator
entry.content:
Introduction

While preparing for modern ABAP topics, I revisited one of the classic concepts we frequently use in reporting programs—control break processing. Most ABAP developers are familiar with statements such as AT NEW, AT END OF, and SUM for calculating subtotals and grand totals. 

Recently, I explored how the same requirement can be implemented using LOOP AT GROUP BY and the REDUCE operator. My goal was to understand whether modern ABAP syntax could make the code more readable and easier to maintain compared to traditional control break statements. 

After implementing both approaches on sample MARA data, I found that the modern syntax significantly reduces the amount of procedural code required for grouping and aggregation. Although the traditional approach is still widely used and perfectly valid, the modern approach felt cleaner and easier to follow, especially when working with complex grouping scenarios. 

In this blog post, I will share the example I created, explain how grouping and subtotal calculations can be achieved using modern ABAP syntax, and highlight some observations from my implementation experience.

Step 1: Define Internal Table Structure

First, create a local structure to hold the required fields from the MARA table. 

Fields used: 

  • MATNR – Material Number 
  • MATKL – Material Group 
  • MTART – Material Type 
  • NTGEW – Net Weight 

An internal table and a variable for storing the grand total are also declared. 

Step 2: Fetch Data from Database

Read the required records from the MARA table into the internal table.

The example fetches:

  • Material Number

  • Material Group

  • Material Type

  • Net Weight

For demonstration purposes, only the first 20 records are selected.

Step 3: Sort the Internal Table

Before grouping, sort the internal table by MATNR.

Sorting ensures that records belonging to the same material are processed together and improves the readability of the output.

Step 4: Group Records Using LOOP AT GROUP BY

Modern ABAP introduces the GROUP BY addition in LOOP statements.

The following grouping logic is used:

  • Each unique MATNR becomes a group.

  • All records having the same MATNR belong to that group.

  • The group object provides access to both the group key and its members.

This eliminates the need for traditional control break statements such as AT NEW and AT END OF.

Step 5: Calculate Subtotals Using REDUCE

The REDUCE operator is used to calculate the subtotal of NTGEW within each group.

Benefits of REDUCE:

  • Compact syntax

  • Improved readability

  • Functional programming approach

  • Eliminates explicit accumulator loops

The subtotal is calculated by iterating through all members of the current group and summing their NTGEW values.

Step 6: Display Group Details

For every material group:

  • Material Number is displayed.

  • Material Group is displayed.

  • Material Type is displayed.

  • Net Weight is displayed.

The LOOP AT GROUP statement is used to access each member belonging to the current group.

Step 7: Display Subtotal

After processing all members of a group:

  • The subtotal of NTGEW is displayed.

  • A separator line is printed using ULINE.

This provides a clear summary for each material group.

Step 8: Calculate Grand Total

The subtotal of each group is added to the grand total variable. 

After all groups are processed, the final accumulated value represents the overall grand total of NTGEW. 

REPORT zrp_control_brk_stt.

TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_mara,
         matnr TYPE matnr,
         matkl TYPE matkl,
         mtart TYPE mtart,
         ntgew TYPE ntgew,
       END OF ty_mara.

DATA: lt_mara        TYPE TABLE OF ty_mara,
      lv_grand_total TYPE ntgew.

SELECT matnr,
       matkl,
       mtart,
       ntgew
  FROM mara
  INTO TABLE @LT_mara
  UP TO 10 ROWS.

IF sy-subrc = 0.
  SORT  lt_mara BY matnr.
ENDIF.

LOOP AT lt_mara ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_mara>)
     GROUP BY ( matnr = <fs_mara>-matnr )
     ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<group>).

  Data(lv_sub_total) = REDUCE ntgew(
                        INIT lv_sum TYPE ntgew
                        FOR wa IN GROUP <group>
                        NEXT lv_sum += wa-ntgew ).

  WRITE: / 'Material:' COLOR 1 , <group>-matnr.

  LOOP AT GROUP <group> ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<member>).

    WRITE: / 'Material Group :-' COLOR 2 , <member>-matkl,
             30 'Material Type :-' COLOR 2, <member>-mtart,
             55 'Net Weight :-' COLOR 2 , <member>-ntgew.
  ENDLOOP.

  WRITE: /  'Subtotal:' COLOR 5, 70 lv_sub_total COLOR 3.

  lv_grand_total += lv_sub_total.

  ULINE.

ENDLOOP.

WRITE: / 'Grand Total:' COLOR 5, 70 lv_grand_total COLOR 5.

Output

The report displays:

  1. Material-wise grouped records

  2. Subtotal for each Material Number

  3. Overall Grand Total

This produces a clean and structured output similar to classical control break processing while using modern ABAP syntax.

Conclusion

After implementing this example, I found that LOOP AT GROUP BY and REDUCE provide a much cleaner alternative to classical control break processing. The biggest advantage for me was eliminating multiple AT NEW and AT END OF blocks while keeping the aggregation logic compact and easy to understand.

From a learning perspective, this exercise helped me better understand ABAP's expression-oriented programming model. While traditional control break statements remain useful when maintaining older applications, I would prefer the GROUP BY and REDUCE approach for new developments because it improves readability and reduces boilerplate code.

I would be interested to know how other ABAP developers are handling subtotal and aggregation requirements in modern ABAP projects and whether they have fully transitioned away from traditional control break statements.

entry.title: HandlingUnit Business Object: Missing Warehouse Value in Key
entry.content:
For one of our production customer in S4Hana public cloud system we have observed that for the HandlingUnit Business Object, the primary key format expects both the HandlingUnitExternalID and the Warehouse number. However, in our system, the Warehouse value is periodically left blank/null, while at other times it is correctly filled.Below are some of the important details w.r.t Business Object.

  • Protocol: OData V4
  • Service: api_handlingunit/srvd_a2x/sap/handlingunit/0001
  • Communication Arrangement: SAP_COM_0364 (Activated on SAP side)
  • Collection: HandlingUnit
  • Key Attributes: "HandlingUnitExternalID" & "Warehouse"

We would like SAP to clarify:

Is HandlingUnitExternalID guaranteed to be globally unique across the entire system on its own, or is the Warehouse value strictly required to ensure uniqueness?

entry.title: Re: Selection Profile - FU Filtering Condition from PO, S...
entry.content:
Hello @rkathir1001 

Note 3065464 - Definition: Basic Shipping vs basic Transportation Management vs advanced Transportation Management, provides "2025_December_Basic vs. Advanced TM.pdf" attachment. The attachment list basic vs. advanced TM functions - it is very useful. 

The attachment doesn't put any restrictions on fields that can be used in selection profiles. 

In general you can use any FU fields that are available either directly or with data crawlers. That is what you can do with standard configuration. But that won't allow you to go from a FU to corresponding orders or deliveries. In order to fetch data from there and provide it to a selection condition you would need to implement a custom data access class. See TM Enhancement Guide, section 4.3 Conditions.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Approach for SAP Cloud ALM feature tester authorizati...
entry.content:
Hi @ThomasMetzger ,

I think your AI is correct. 
Take a look at this help page. Here you find the confirmation that role 'imp_cdm_Tester' actually includes the authorization to change the status to "Suc. Tested":
https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/setup-administration/role-templates

 The new partner functions were added recently in the Feature process and it seems that not all documentation was updated 100% yet. There is actually a good matrix which describes which role(s) is needed for which status change:
https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/role-collection-recommendations?locale=en-US
But the "Tester" scenario with role imp_cdm_Tester was not yet included in the matrix.. But there is a comment about the "Tester":
"
With the Tester role template imp_cdm_Tester, you can only switch the status of features to Successfully Tested. You have to create a custom role collection including another role template or collection, which at least provides access to the Features app. For example, you can combine the Tester role template with the Project Member role collection.
"


--> so just create a custom role collection, add the role imp_cdm_Tester, and imp_pjm_ProjectMember and assign the custom role collection to your testers in SAP Cloud ALM and verify if it works (don't forget to assign the users to the CALM project).

Thanks,
Philipp

entry.title: Migration of Solman to CALM - How to use the EarlyWatch Alert
entry.content:
We are planning to migrate from SAP Solution Manager (SolMan) to SAP Cloud ALM (CALM).

Currently, SolMan is being used only for EarlyWatch Alert (EWA) reporting and Java system monitoring, and we are not utilizing it for any other activities.

Could you please confirm whether, after decommissioning SolMan, we will still be able to access EarlyWatch Alert reports through SAP for Me? If so, are there any prerequisites or retention considerations that we should be aware of before proceeding with the shutdown?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Migration of Solman to CALM - How to use the EarlyWat...
entry.content:
Dear customer,

kindly review the following documentation:

  • 3427582 - SAP Solution Manager features and functionality in SAP Cloud ALM
  • 1257308 - FAQ: Using EarlyWatch Alert

EarlyWatch Alert for ABAP based systems and Hana DB can be processed on SAP4Me:

  • Direct Transfer of EWA service data from the SAP ABAP based system to SAP
  • If you run HANA native applications - this is a HANA system without an ABAP system on top - HANA Cockpit transfers the EWA data to SAP

For non-ABAP systems like Java based ones there is no possibility to generate the EWA on SAP4Me, besides using a Focused Run (FRUN) system.

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Prevent cross client transports imported multiple times when using transport target group
entry.content:
Hi all,

we have the following setup (sorry, for not pasting a screenshot)

DEV (010) --> /DEVGRP/ --------> QAS (010)
                       \  \
                       |   \
                       /    \  
DEV (080) <------------      --> QAS (080)

Our Idea is to do all the customizing and development in DEV.010 and route back customizing (all client dependent stuff) via transport group into DEV.080 as well as using the group to fill QAS.010 and QAS.080 at the same time.

My Question is:

  • Can we somehow prevent to double import cross-client transports (e.g. repository) multiple times into the systems e.g. into QAS.010 and QAS.080 at the same time?
  • Would it especially hurt in our setup that we "backport" the TRs also into DEV where we might hit locked objects or even introduce downgrades when "backporting"?

PS: I looked at https://me.sap.com/notes/0002960504 but it seems to gloss over my concerns (but maybe I'm overly concerned)

Many thanks / cheers

Jens

entry.title: Re: Managing JMS Queues Programmatically with the new SAP Cloud Integration JMS OData API
entry.content:
Hi @MarcoKoch,

Thanks for the blog, it's a useful description of a long-awaited functionality. However:

You need a Process Integration Runtime service instance with the integration-flow plan and the OAuth credentials from its service binding. All endpoints live under /api/v1/ on your tenant URL, and authentication uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials.

Are you sure this isn't an "api" plan instead of an "integration-flow" one? My calls are successful using the API plan, but fail with the error "Requested route ('<tenant-specific-details>.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com') does not exist." if I go for the integration-flow plan.

This thread has helped me during troubleshooting:
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/unable-to-access-cloud-integration-tenant-apis-like-messageprocessinglogs/qaq-p/14003899

entry.title: Re: API PRODHIERPRODS_0001 – POST returns ;Create operations are disabled for association;
entry.content: Hello, thank you for your question. It has been forwarded to the experts. Kind regards, Henrike

entry.title: Re: Supply chain management
entry.content:
On the face of it SD and MM might seem simpler and easier than EWM. But if you dig deeper SD and MM are very tightly integrated with other ERP components like financials and controlling, stock management, MRP planning, etc. Thus any SD or MM consultant will have to grasp those integrations eventually and understand how they work together. I can't see how a senior SD or MM consultant could work efficiently without such a knowledge. 

In contrast, to me EWM is very self contained in regards to other ERP modules. It even uses qRFC communication to integrate deliveries. Therefore it is straightforward to see the boundaries of what EWM consultants do. That the same time EWM seems more complex at the beginning.

entry.title: SAP ABAP Data Dictionary :A Complete Beginner's Guide to Domains, Data Elements, and Database Tables
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP ABAP development, one of the most fundamental and frequently used components is the Data Dictionary (DDIC). Whether you are creating reports, SmartForms, ALV reports, interfaces, or custom applications, DDIC plays a critical role in defining and managing data consistently across the SAP landscape.

The SAP Data Dictionary serves as a centralized repository that stores metadata definitions for database objects. It helps developers maintain data integrity, improve reusability, and ensure consistency throughout the system.

This article provides a comprehensive overview of SAP Data Dictionary concepts, including Domains, Data Elements, Database Tables, Structures, Table Types, and Technical Settings. By the end of this guide, you will have a solid understanding of DDIC and its importance in SAP ABAP development.

What is SAP Data Dictionary (DDIC)?

SAP Data Dictionary (DDIC) is a central repository where all data definitions and metadata are maintained.

It acts as a bridge between SAP applications and the underlying database by providing standardized definitions for business data.

Key Benefits of DDIC

✔ Centralized Data Management

✔ Data Consistency Across Applications

✔ Reusability of Data Objects

✔ Improved Performance

✔ Reduced Development Effort

✔ Database Independence

Execute T-code or Path

From the SAP Easy Access Screen: execute transaction code SE11 or follow the SAP menu path and Execute: SE11 ABAP Dictionary

Transaction codeSE11
Menu PathSAP Menu > Tools > ABAP Workbench > Development > SE11 – ABAP Dictionary

 

DDIC Architecture Overview

The relationship between DDIC objects follows a hierarchical structure:

Domain
   ↓
Data Element
   ↓
Table Field
   ↓
Database Table

 

Domain & How to create a Domain?

A Domain defines the technical attributes of a field.

It specifies:

  • Data Type
  • Length
  • Decimal Places
  • Value Range
  • Fixed Values

Think of a Domain as a blueprint that determines how data should be stored in the database.

  • Choose the Domain radio button
  • Give the domain name that you want to create.

Enter the short description of the domain. You can maintain text up to 60 characters.

On domain definition, you need to maintain the format details of the data type and length.

After providing all the required details for the domain, click on the save button.

Select the local object to save the created domain in an object directory.

Check Syntax & Activate.

Click Activate (Ctrl+F3) to activate the Domain.

Advantages of Domains

  • Eliminates redundant definitions
  • Improves consistency
  • Easy maintenance
  • Reusable across multiple applications

Data Element & How to create a Data Element?

A Data Element provides business meaning to a field.

While a Domain defines technical properties, a Data Element explains what the field represents from a business perspective.

Data Element Contains

  • Field Labels
  • Documentation
  • Business Description
  • Domain Reference

The Domain controls storage and validation, while the Data Element tells users that the field represents Employee Status.

  • Choose the Data Element radio button.
  • Enter the Data Element name that you want to create.

As we are creating data element, select data element radio button and press enter.

  1. Update the short description of the Data Element.
  2. Select the predefined radio button and update the data type, and length fields.

Text information is updated in field label tab, click on field label and update the following information.

  •  Click on the save button (ctrl+s) to save the configured data element in a package. You get a message as “ZDATA_ELEMENT_EMP saved.
  • Click Check (Ctrl+F2) and then Activate (Ctrl+F3).

Domain vs Data Element

DomainData Element
Technical DefinitionBusiness Definition
Data Type & LengthLabels & Documentation
Validation RulesUser Understanding
ReusableReusable

Database Tables & How to create a table?

Database Tables are used to store application data physically in the database.

Every business transaction ultimately stores information in one or more database tables.

Components of a Table

  • Fields
  • Primary Key
  • Technical Settings
  • Delivery Class
  • Foreign Keys

On the initial screen, select the database table option and enter the name of the database table that you want to be created. After updating name click on create button. Here we are creating ZTEST_EMP table.

On the next screen, update the following data. By default delivery and maintenance tab is selected.

  1. Short Description: Update the short description of the database table.
  2. Delivery Class: Update the delivery class from the list, you can get the list be clicking the search option (F4).
  3. Data Browser/ Table View Maintain: Select the display/maintenance allowed from the drop down list.

Select field tab and update the following data.

  1. Field: Update the field name in the field column (16 characters)
  2. Key: Select the key option if you want the field as table key.
  3. Data Element: Update the data element key from the list.

Click on save button to save the configured data. You get a message as ZTEST_EMP saved.

Types of SAP Tables

1. Transparent Tables

Most commonly used table type.

Characteristics:

  • One SAP table corresponds to one database table.
  • Data is physically stored.

Examples:

MARA
KNA1
LFA1

2. Pooled Tables

Used to store small amounts of data.

Multiple logical tables share one physical database table.

3. Cluster Tables

Store related records together.

Primarily used in older SAP systems.

Note:

In SAP S/4HANA, Transparent Tables are predominantly used.

 

Key Takeaways

• Domain defines technical properties.
• Data Element provides business meaning.
• Database Tables store application data physically.
• DDIC ensures consistency and reusability across SAP applications.

 

Conclusion

SAP Data Dictionary (DDIC) serves as the foundation of SAP ABAP development by providing centralized definitions for business data. Understanding Domains, Data Elements, and Database Tables is essential for building scalable and maintainable SAP applications.

A strong understanding of DDIC not only helps developers create efficient solutions but also plays a significant role in SAP ABAP interviews and real-world project implementations.

**In the next article, we will explore Structures, Table Types, and Technical Settings in detail.**

entry.title: SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise: beyond the announcement, toward the architecture
entry.content:
When SAP says it’s becoming a ‘Business AI company,’ that’s not marketing. It’s a structural admission that the ERP paradigm built around systems of record is being replaced by something that executes on your behalf.

Here’s what I find most interesting as a technical architect, and what I think gets underplayed.

The architecture

Most coverage focuses on Joule, the 224 agents, and the Claude partnership. These are components. What matters is how they stack.

Figure 1: SAP Joule Architecture Stack

SAP isn’t trying to build the world’s best AI model. It’s building an AI-powered enterprise operating system. An Anthropic partnership makes sense because the roles are distinct: Claude handles reasoning, and SAP handles domain intelligence and governance.

“For the mission-critical processes of our customers, ‘almost right’ just isn’t good enough.” Quote by Christian Klein, CEO, SAP

What the Autonomous Finance demo actually tells us

The most concrete example from Sapphire: a CFO asks Joule to prepare a bank briefing. Minutes later, a completed presentation surfaces with live data, flagged risks, and analysis. That used to take hours across multiple people.

That’s a testable claim, which is the right kind of proof point for enterprise buyers. The same logic applies across Autonomous Spend and Autonomous Supply Chain.

The Autonomous Close Assistant compresses the financial close from weeks to days by automating journal entries, reconciliation, and error resolution. In financial systems with regulatory requirements, there’s no tolerance for ‘good enough.’ That’s what makes the SAP Knowledge Graph central here. It’s not just retrieving data. It’s giving agents the business context to act correctly.

How SAP solved the governance problem

What makes this architecture different from previous enterprise AI attempts is the governed execution layer SAP built around the agents. Joule doesn’t connect directly to agents. Every request routes through the SAP Agent Gateway, which handles identity authentication, principal propagation, and policy enforcement before any agent acts. Agents can collaborate via Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol, but every A2A interaction is routed through that gateway. No agent acts outside the governed path. No identity is lost between hops. Every interaction is auditable.n enterprise software, that last point determines whether a CFO will actually sign off on autonomous finance processes.

Figure 2: SAP Joule — Joule as orchestrator, SAP Agent Gateway, A2A protocol, and end-to-end governed flow

The SAP and NVIDIA collaboration on Joule Studio runtime adds another layer. NVIDIA OpenShell provides isolated execution environments, filesystem and network policy enforcement, and runtime containment. SAP contributes the enterprise governance side: roles, identity lifecycle, policy semantics, observability. OpenShell answers ‘Can this agent action safely execute?’ Joule Studio runtime answers ‘Should this action happen at all?’ SAP is a key contributor to the OpenShell open source project. The Joule Studio Early Adopter Program is open now.

What this means for the SAP architect

The required skill set is shifting. The next generation of SAP architects won’t train foundation models, but they will need to understand how agentic systems fit into existing business process logic.

Figure 4: SAP Architect Skill Stack — core platform, agentic layer, AI fundamentals

Agent mining is one of the less-discussed announcements worth paying attention to. It’s an extension of process mining: it catalogs what agents did, where they slowed down, and whether they behaved as expected. The AI Agent Hub, included free for all SAP Business AI Platform customers, is where this governance lives. As more agents start operating inside finance and supply chain, this observability is not optional.

Functional expertise in finance, supply chain, and procurement doesn’t get less valuable here. Someone has to define what ‘correct’ looks like for an agent operating in those domains. The architect who can connect deep process knowledge with agentic design patterns will be at the center of these transformations.

My take

SAP Sapphire 2026 moved the enterprise AI conversation from roadmap to production intent. The partnerships with Anthropic, AWS, NVIDIA, and Google Cloud are real. The domain depth built from 50 years of ERP process logic is a genuine differentiator that general-purpose AI platforms will find hard to replicate.

Figure 3: SAP Autonomous Enterprise — Autonomous Suite, Joule Work, Business AI Platform and key partners

SAP has also been serious about the data foundation underneath the agents. The Dremio acquisition makes SAP Business Data Cloud an Apache Iceberg-native lakehouse for SAP and non-SAP data. Prior Labs brings tabular foundation modelling in-house. Reltio adds master data management. SAP-RPT-1.5 is a relational pretrained transformer built for structured enterprise data. The model is a commodity layer. The data and context SAP has assembled are not.

What SAP has built is the coordination layer between AI systems and the systems of record they need to operate inside. That’s been the missing piece in enterprise AI for two years. Joule Studio, the Agent Gateway, agent mining via the AI Agent Hub, and the NVIDIA OpenShell runtime together answer the question most agentic initiatives couldn’t: not whether an agent can act, but whether you can prove it was allowed to.

One question I keep sitting with: will enterprise AI be won by the best model, or by the platform that best combines business context, governance, and execution? I suspect it’s the latter. SAP is making a credible bet on exactly that.

#SAP  #SAPPHIRE2026  #AutonomousEnterprise  #EnterpriseAI  #Joule  #SAPBTP  #AgenticAI  #DigitalTransformation

entry.title: Beyond Basic AI Error Handling in SAP CPI - A Rules-First Architecture with HANA Cloud Learning
entry.content:
The Problem Every Integration Developer Knows

Every SAP CPI developer has lived this scenario.

An iFlow fails in production at an inconvenient hour. You open the MPL log, stare at a cryptic stack trace, and spend the next 30–45 minutes manually cross-referencing adapter configurations, checking credentials, and testing payloads. By the time you identify the root cause  a null field in a mapping, an expired OAuth token, a malformed XML payload  the fix takes five minutes. The diagnosis took an hour.

Recent SAP Community posts have explored using the AI Adapter to automate this diagnosis, and the concept is genuinely sound. But the most common approach  sending the raw exception directly to an LLM has five problems that make it unsuitable for real production environments:

ProblemReal Impact
Raw payload sent to LLMBusiness data leaves the tenant — GDPR risk
Every error hits the LLMAuth failures and timeouts: unnecessary API cost at scale
No deduplicationSame error 1000 times = 1000 API calls
Free-text responseCannot be routed, filtered, or acted on programmatically
Response goes back to senderDevelopers never see the diagnosis

This post describes a different architecture — one that classifies errors deterministically first, sanitizes all business data before any AI call, caches intelligence in SAP HANA Cloud, and uses AI only for errors that are genuinely novel.

In my testing: 3 of 4 error types never touched the LLM at all.

What I Built: The ErrorAnalyzer Agent

The ErrorAnalyzer Agent is a native SAP CPI package  two iFlows, 13 Groovy scripts, a HANA Cloud free-tier instance, and the SAP AI Adapter configured with a breakthrough setting that is completely undocumented in SAP's official documentation.

Package contents:

  • EA_TestHarness — the main analyser iFlow, triggered via ProcessDirect
  • EA_VictimFlow — an example source iFlow that deliberately triggers errors to demonstrate the full pipeline

Technologies used:

  • SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)  iFlows, Groovy scripts, JDBC adapter, AI Adapter, Mail adapter
  • SAP HANA Cloud  free tier, with NLP vector engine enabled for V2
  • OpenRouter  LLM gateway (free tier models available)
  • OpenAI  via OpenRouter for AI diagnosis

Architecture Overview

The diagram below shows the complete three-zone architecture. Zone 1 handles detection inside SAP CPI. Zone 2 contains the intelligence layer — classification, sanitization, and dedup logic. Zone 3 is the AI and persistence layer.

 

 

The key design principle: rules first, AI only when necessary. Known errors (auth failures, timeouts, malformed payloads) are handled deterministically in milliseconds with zero LLM cost. Only genuinely novel or complex errors reach Claude.

Prerequisites

Before building, ensure you have the following:

  • SAP Integration Suite trial or production tenant with Cloud Integration capability enabled
  • OpenRouter account  free tier available at openrouter.ai (no credit card required for free models)
  • SAP HANA Cloud free tier instance provisioned on BTP (covered in setup steps below)
  • Gmail or SMTP account for email notifications
  • Postman or any REST client for testing

Step 1 — Security Material Setup

Before configuring any adapter, create two security artifacts in CPI Monitor.

1a. OpenRouter API Key

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → Secure Parameter

Field Value

NameOpenRouter_API_Key
TypeSecure Parameter
ValueBearer sk-or-v1-xxxxxxxxxx    ← include "Bearer " prefix — this is critical

Click Deploy. Wait for status to show Deployed  before proceeding.

Important: Do not store only the API key. Store the complete value including the "Bearer " prefix with a space. The AI Adapter uses this as the entire Authorization header value. This is part of the undocumented configuration covered in detail in Step 4.
